title: Dual-core

infobox.value: (**MCP**) is a [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") on a single [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC) with two or more separate [central processing units](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPUs), called *cores* to emphasize their multiplicity (for example, *dual-core* or *quad-core*). Each core reads and executes [program instructions](/wiki/Instruction_set "Instruction set"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) specifically ordinary [CPU instructions](/wiki/Instruction_set "Instruction set") (such as add, move data, and branch). However, the MCP can run instructions on separate cores at the same time, increasing overall speed for programs that support [multithreading](/wiki/Multithreading_(computer_architecture) "Multithreading (computer architecture)") or other [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") techniques.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Manufacturers typically integrate the cores onto a single IC [die](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)"), known as a **chip multiprocessor** (**CMP**), or onto multiple dies in a single [chip package](/wiki/Chip_carrier "Chip carrier"). As of 2024, the microprocessors used in almost all new [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") are multi-core.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Dual-core](/w/index.php?title=Dual-core&redirect=no "Dual-core"))

Microprocessor with more than one processing unit

"Dual Core" redirects here; not to be confused with [Dual Core (hip hop duo)](/wiki/Dual_Core_(hip_hop_duo) "Dual Core (hip hop duo)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Dual_Core_Generic.svg/250px-Dual_Core_Generic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dual_Core_Generic.svg)

Diagram of a generic dual-core processor with CPU-local level-1 caches and a shared, on-die level-2 cache

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/E6750bs8.jpg/250px-E6750bs8.jpg)](/wiki/File:E6750bs8.jpg)

The Intel [Core 2 Duo](/wiki/Core_2_Duo "Core 2 Duo") E6750 was an early dual-core processor.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Athlon64x2-6400plus.jpg/250px-Athlon64x2-6400plus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Athlon64x2-6400plus.jpg)

An AMD [Athlon X2 6400+](/wiki/Athlon_64_X2 "Athlon 64 X2") dual-core processor

A **multi-core processor** (**MCP**) is a [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") on a single [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC) with two or more separate [central processing units](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPUs), called *cores* to emphasize their multiplicity (for example, *dual-core* or *quad-core*). Each core reads and executes [program instructions](/wiki/Instruction_set "Instruction set"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) specifically ordinary [CPU instructions](/wiki/Instruction_set "Instruction set") (such as add, move data, and branch). However, the MCP can run instructions on separate cores at the same time, increasing overall speed for programs that support [multithreading](/wiki/Multithreading_(computer_architecture) "Multithreading (computer architecture)") or other [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") techniques.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Manufacturers typically integrate the cores onto a single IC [die](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)"), known as a **chip multiprocessor** (**CMP**), or onto multiple dies in a single [chip package](/wiki/Chip_carrier "Chip carrier"). As of 2024, the microprocessors used in almost all new [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") are multi-core.

A multi-core processor implements [multiprocessing](/wiki/Multiprocessing "Multiprocessing") in a single physical package. Designers may couple cores in a multi-core device tightly or loosely. For example, cores may or may not share [caches](/wiki/CPU_cache "CPU cache"), and they may implement [message passing](/wiki/Message_passing "Message passing") or [shared-memory](/wiki/Shared_memory "Shared memory") inter-core communication methods. Common [network topologies](/wiki/Network_topology "Network topology") used to interconnect cores include [bus](/wiki/Bus_network "Bus network"), [ring](/wiki/Ring_network "Ring network"), two-dimensional [mesh](/wiki/Mesh_networking "Mesh networking"), and [crossbar](/wiki/Crossbar_switch "Crossbar switch"). Homogeneous multi-core systems include only identical cores; [heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogeneous_computing "Heterogeneous computing") multi-core systems have cores that are not identical (e.g. [big.LITTLE](/wiki/ARM_big.LITTLE "ARM big.LITTLE") have heterogeneous cores that share the same [instruction set](/wiki/Instruction_set_architecture "Instruction set architecture"), while [AMD Accelerated Processing Units](/wiki/AMD_Accelerated_Processing_Unit "AMD Accelerated Processing Unit") have cores that do not share the same instruction set). Just as with single-processor systems, cores in multi-core systems may implement architectures such as [VLIW](/wiki/Very_long_instruction_word "Very long instruction word"), [superscalar](/wiki/Superscalar_processor "Superscalar processor"), [vector](/wiki/Vector_processor "Vector processor"), or [multithreading](/wiki/Multithreading_(computer_architecture) "Multithreading (computer architecture)").

Multi-core processors are widely used across many application domains, including [general-purpose](/wiki/Computer "Computer"), [embedded](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system"), [network](/wiki/Network_processor "Network processor"), [digital signal processing](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing") (DSP), and [graphics](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit") (GPU). Core count goes up to even dozens, and for specialized chips over 10,000,[[3]](#cite_note-10496_CUDA_cores-3) and in [supercomputers](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") (i.e. clusters of chips) the count can go over 10 million (and in [one case](/wiki/Gyoukou "Gyoukou") up to 20 million processing elements total in addition to host processors).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The improvement in performance gained by the use of a multi-core processor depends very much on the [software](/wiki/Software "Software") algorithms used and their implementation. In particular, possible gains are limited by the fraction of the software that can [run in parallel](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") simultaneously on multiple cores; this effect is described by [Amdahl's law](/wiki/Amdahl%27s_law "Amdahl's law"). In the best case, so-called [embarrassingly parallel](/wiki/Embarrassingly_parallel "Embarrassingly parallel") problems may realize speedup factors near the number of cores, or even more if the problem is split up enough to fit within each core's cache(s), avoiding use of much slower main-system memory. Most applications, however, are not accelerated as much unless programmers invest effort in [refactoring](/wiki/Refactoring "Refactoring").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The parallelization of software is a significant ongoing topic of research. Cointegration of multiprocessor applications provides flexibility in network architecture design. Adaptability within parallel models is an additional feature of systems utilizing these protocols.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In the consumer market, dual-core processors (that is, microprocessors with two units) started becoming commonplace on personal computers in the late 2000s.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the early 2010s, quad-core processors were also being adopted in that era for higher-end systems before becoming standard by the mid 2010s. In the late 2010s, hexa-core (six cores) started entering the mainstream[[8]](#cite_note-8) and since the early 2020s has overtaken quad-core in many spaces.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The terms *multi-core* and *dual-core* most commonly refer to [central processing units](/wiki/Central_processing_units "Central processing units") (CPUs), but are sometimes applied to [digital signal processors](/wiki/Digital_signal_processors "Digital signal processors") (DSPs) and [systems on chip](/wiki/Systems_on_chip "Systems on chip") (SoCs). The terms are generally used only to refer to multi-core microprocessors that are manufactured on the *same* integrated circuit [die](/wiki/Die_(integrated_circuit) "Die (integrated circuit)"); separate microprocessor dies in the same package are generally referred to by another name, such as *[multi-chip module](/wiki/Multi-chip_module "Multi-chip module")*. This article uses the terms "multi-core" and "dual-core" for CPUs manufactured on the *same* integrated circuit, unless otherwise noted.

In contrast to multi-core systems, the term *multi-CPU* refers to multiple physically separate processing-units (which often contain special circuitry to facilitate communication between each other).

The terms *[many-core](/wiki/Manycore_processor "Manycore processor")* and *massively multi-core* are sometimes used to describe multi-core architectures with an especially high number of cores (tens to thousands[[10]](#cite_note-10)).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Some systems use many [soft microprocessor](/wiki/Soft_microprocessor "Soft microprocessor") cores placed on a single [FPGA](/wiki/Field-programmable_gate_array "Field-programmable gate array"). Each "core" can be considered a "[semiconductor intellectual property core](/wiki/Semiconductor_intellectual_property_core "Semiconductor intellectual property core")" as well as a CPU core.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development")]

While manufacturing technology improves, reducing the size of individual gates, physical limits of [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor")-based [microelectronics](/wiki/Microelectronics "Microelectronics") have become a major design concern. These physical limitations can cause significant heat dissipation and data synchronization problems. Various other methods are used to improve CPU performance. Some [instruction-level parallelism](/wiki/Instruction-level_parallelism "Instruction-level parallelism") (ILP) methods such as [superscalar](/wiki/Superscalar "Superscalar") [pipelining](/wiki/Instruction_pipelining "Instruction pipelining") are suitable for many applications, but are inefficient for others that contain difficult-to-predict code. Many applications are better suited to [thread-level parallelism](/wiki/Thread-level_parallelism "Thread-level parallelism") (TLP) methods, and multiple independent CPUs are commonly used to increase a system's overall TLP. A combination of increased available space (due to refined manufacturing processes) and the demand for increased TLP led to the development of multi-core CPUs.

### Apple's Scorpius CPU

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Apple's Scorpius CPU")]

In 1985, Apple engineer Sam Holland convinced Apple to develop its own 4-processor CPU chip to power future Macs[[12]](#cite_note-12) called Scorpius.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The processor's proposed feature set was highly ambitious, including four cores and SIMD (vector) support with inter-processor communication features.[[14]](#cite_note-14) With the design beyond the fabrication capabilities of the time, the project was brought to an end in 1989.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Early innovations: the Stanford Hydra project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early innovations: the Stanford Hydra project")]

In the 1990s, [Kunle Olukotun](/wiki/Kunle_Olukotun "Kunle Olukotun") led the Stanford Hydra Chip Multiprocessor (CMP) research project. This initiative was among the first to demonstrate the viability of integrating multiple processors on a single chip, a concept that laid the groundwork for today's multicore processors. The Hydra project introduced support for thread-level speculation (TLS), enabling more efficient parallel execution of programs.

### Commercial incentives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Commercial incentives")]

Several business motives drive the development of multi-core architectures. For decades, it was possible to improve performance of a CPU by shrinking the area of the integrated circuit (IC), which reduced the cost per device on the IC. Alternatively, for the same circuit area, more transistors could be used in the design, which increased functionality, especially for [complex instruction set computing](/wiki/Complex_instruction_set_computing "Complex instruction set computing") (CISC) architectures. [Clock rates](/wiki/Clock_rate "Clock rate") also increased by orders of magnitude in the decades of the late 20th century, from several megahertz in the 1980s to several gigahertz in the early 2000s.

As the rate of clock speed improvements slowed, increased use of parallel computing in the form of multi-core processors has been pursued to improve overall processing performance. Multiple cores were used on the same CPU chip, which could then lead to better sales of CPU chips with two or more cores. For example, [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") has produced a 48-core processor for research in cloud computing; each core has an [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") architecture.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Technical factors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Technical factors")]

Since computer manufacturers have long implemented [symmetric multiprocessing](/wiki/Symmetric_multiprocessing "Symmetric multiprocessing") (SMP) designs using discrete CPUs, the issues regarding implementing multi-core processor architecture and supporting it with software are well known.

Additionally:

* Using a proven processing-core design without architectural changes reduces design risk significantly.
* For general-purpose processors, much of the motivation for multi-core processors comes from greatly diminished gains in processor performance from increasing the [operating frequency](/wiki/Frequency_scaling "Frequency scaling"). This is due to three primary factors:[[18]](#cite_note-18)
  1. The *memory wall*; the increasing gap between processor and memory speeds. This, in effect, pushes for cache sizes to be larger in order to mask the latency of memory. This helps only to the extent that memory bandwidth is not the bottleneck in performance.
  2. The *ILP wall*; the increasing difficulty of finding enough [parallelism in a single instruction stream](/wiki/Instruction-level_parallelism "Instruction-level parallelism") to keep a high-performance single-core processor busy.
  3. The *power wall*; the trend of consuming exponentially increasing power (and thus also generating exponentially increasing heat) with each factorial increase of operating frequency. This increase can be mitigated by "[shrinking](/wiki/Die_shrink "Die shrink")" the processor by using smaller traces for the same logic. The *power wall* poses manufacturing, system design and deployment problems that have not been justified in the face of the diminished gains in performance due to the *memory wall* and *ILP wall*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In order to continue delivering regular performance improvements for general-purpose processors, manufacturers such as [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") and [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") have turned to multi-core designs, sacrificing lower manufacturing-costs for higher performance in some applications and systems. Multi-core architectures are being developed, but so are the alternatives. An especially strong contender for established markets is the further integration of peripheral functions into the chip.

### Advantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Advantages")]

The proximity of multiple CPU cores on the same die allows the [cache coherency](/wiki/Cache_coherency "Cache coherency") circuitry to operate at a much higher clock rate than what is possible if the signals have to travel off-chip. Combining equivalent CPUs on a single die significantly improves the performance of [cache snoop](/wiki/Cache_snooping "Cache snooping") (alternative: [Bus snooping](/wiki/Bus_snooping "Bus snooping")) operations. Put simply, this means that [signals](/wiki/Discrete_signal "Discrete signal") between different CPUs travel shorter distances, and therefore those signals [degrade](/wiki/Degradation_(telecommunications) "Degradation (telecommunications)") less. These higher-quality signals allow more data to be sent in a given time period, since individual signals can be shorter and do not need to be repeated as often.

Assuming that the die can physically fit into the package, multi-core CPU designs require much less [printed circuit board](/wiki/Printed_circuit_board "Printed circuit board") (PCB) space than do multi-chip [SMP](/wiki/Symmetric_multiprocessing "Symmetric multiprocessing") designs. Also, a dual-core processor uses slightly less power than two coupled single-core processors, principally because of the decreased power required to drive signals external to the chip. Furthermore, the cores share some circuitry, like the L2 cache and the interface to the [front-side bus](/wiki/Front-side_bus "Front-side bus") (FSB). In terms of competing technologies for the available silicon die area, multi-core design can make use of proven CPU core library designs and produce a product with lower risk of design error than devising a new wider-core design. Also, adding more cache suffers from diminishing returns.

Multi-core chips also allow higher performance at lower energy. This can be a big factor in mobile devices that operate on batteries. Since each core in a multi-core CPU is generally more energy-efficient, the chip becomes more efficient than having a single large monolithic core. This allows higher performance with less energy. A challenge in this, however, is the additional overhead of writing parallel code.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Disadvantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Disadvantages")]

Maximizing the usage of the computing resources provided by multi-core processors requires adjustments both to the [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") (OS) support and to existing application software. Also, the ability of multi-core processors to increase application performance depends on the use of multiple threads within applications.

Integration of a multi-core chip can lower the chip production yields. They are also more difficult to manage thermally than lower-density single-core designs. Intel has partially countered this first problem by creating its quad-core designs by combining two dual-core ones on a single die with a unified cache, hence any two working dual-core dies can be used, as opposed to producing four cores on a single die and requiring all four to work to produce a quad-core CPU. From an architectural point of view, ultimately, single CPU designs may make better use of the silicon surface area than multiprocessing cores, so a development commitment to this architecture may carry the risk of obsolescence. Finally, raw processing power is not the only constraint on system performance. Two processing cores sharing the same system bus and memory bandwidth limits the real-world performance advantage.

## Hardware

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hardware")]

### Trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Trends")]

The trend in processor development has been towards an ever-increasing number of cores, as processors with hundreds or even thousands of cores become theoretically possible.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In addition, multi-core chips mixed with [simultaneous multithreading](/wiki/Simultaneous_multithreading "Simultaneous multithreading"), memory-on-chip, and special-purpose ["heterogeneous"](/wiki/Heterogeneous_computing "Heterogeneous computing") (or asymmetric) cores promise further performance and efficiency gains, especially in processing multimedia, recognition and networking applications. For example, a [big.LITTLE](/wiki/ARM_big.LITTLE "ARM big.LITTLE") core includes a high-performance core (called 'big') and a low-power core (called 'LITTLE'). There is also a trend towards improving energy-efficiency by focusing on performance-per-watt with advanced fine-grain or ultra fine-grain [power management](/wiki/Power_management "Power management") and dynamic [voltage](/wiki/Dynamic_voltage_scaling "Dynamic voltage scaling") and [frequency scaling](/wiki/Dynamic_frequency_scaling "Dynamic frequency scaling") (i.e. [laptop](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") computers and [portable media players](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player")).

Chips designed from the outset for a large number of cores (rather than having evolved from single core designs) are sometimes referred to as [manycore](/wiki/Manycore_processor "Manycore processor") designs, emphasising qualitative differences.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Architecture")]

The composition and balance of the cores in multi-core architecture show great variety. Some architectures use one core design repeated consistently ("homogeneous"), while others use a mixture of different cores, each optimized for a different, "[heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogeneous_computing "Heterogeneous computing")" role.

How multiple cores are implemented and integrated significantly affects both the developer's programming skills and the consumer's expectations of apps and interactivity versus the device.[[21]](#cite_note-21) A device advertised as being octa-core will only have independent cores if advertised as *True Octa-core*, or similar styling, as opposed to being merely two sets of quad-cores each with fixed clock speeds.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The article "CPU designers debate multi-core future" by Rick Merritt, EE Times 2008,[[24]](#cite_note-24) includes these comments:

> Chuck Moore [...] suggested computers should be like cellphones, using a variety of specialty cores to run modular software scheduled by a high-level applications programming interface.
>
> [...] Atsushi Hasegawa, a senior chief engineer at [Renesas](/wiki/Renesas "Renesas"), generally agreed. He suggested the cellphone's use of many specialty cores working in concert is a good model for future multi-core designs.
>
> [...] [Anant Agarwal](/wiki/Anant_Agarwal "Anant Agarwal"), founder and chief executive of startup [Tilera](/wiki/Tilera "Tilera"), took the opposing view. He said multi-core chips need to be homogeneous collections of general-purpose cores to keep the software model simple.

## Software effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Software effects")]

An outdated version of an anti-virus application may create a new thread for a scan process, while its [GUI](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") thread waits for commands from the user (e.g. cancel the scan). In such cases, a multi-core architecture is of little benefit for the application itself due to the single thread doing all the heavy lifting and the inability to balance the work evenly across multiple cores. Programming truly multithreaded code often requires complex co-ordination of threads and can easily introduce subtle and difficult-to-find bugs due to the interweaving of processing on data shared between threads (see [thread-safety](/wiki/Thread-safety "Thread-safety")). Consequently, such code is much more difficult to debug than single-threaded code when it breaks. There has been a perceived lack of motivation for writing consumer-level threaded applications because of the relative rarity of consumer-level demand for maximum use of computer hardware. Also, serial tasks like decoding the [entropy encoding](/wiki/Entropy_encoding "Entropy encoding") algorithms used in [video codecs](/wiki/Video_codec "Video codec") are impossible to parallelize because each result generated is used to help create the next result of the entropy decoding algorithm.

Given the increasing emphasis on multi-core chip design, stemming from the grave thermal and power consumption problems posed by any further significant increase in processor clock speeds, the extent to which software can be multithreaded to take advantage of these new chips is likely to be the single greatest constraint on computer performance in the future. If developers are unable to design software to fully exploit the resources provided by multiple cores, then they will ultimately reach an insurmountable performance ceiling.

The telecommunications market had been one of the first that needed a new design of parallel datapath packet processing because there was a very quick adoption of these multiple-core processors for the datapath and the control plane. These MPUs are going to replace[[25]](#cite_note-25) the traditional Network Processors that were based on proprietary [microcode](/wiki/Microcode "Microcode") or [picocode](/wiki/Picocode "Picocode").

[Parallel programming](/wiki/Parallel_programming "Parallel programming") techniques can benefit from multiple cores directly. Some existing [parallel programming models](/wiki/Parallel_programming_model "Parallel programming model") such as [Cilk Plus](/wiki/Cilk_Plus "Cilk Plus"), [OpenMP](/wiki/OpenMP "OpenMP"), [OpenHMPP](/wiki/OpenHMPP "OpenHMPP"), [FastFlow](/wiki/Algorithmic_skeleton#FastFlow "Algorithmic skeleton"), Skandium, [MPI](/wiki/Message_Passing_Interface "Message Passing Interface"), and [Erlang](/wiki/Erlang_(programming_language) "Erlang (programming language)") can be used on multi-core platforms. Intel introduced a new abstraction for C++ parallelism called [TBB](/wiki/Threading_Building_Blocks "Threading Building Blocks"). Other research efforts include the [Codeplay Sieve System](/wiki/Sieve_C%2B%2B_Parallel_Programming_System "Sieve C++ Parallel Programming System"), Cray's [Chapel](/wiki/Chapel_(programming_language) "Chapel (programming language)"), Sun's [Fortress](/wiki/Fortress_programming_language "Fortress programming language"), and IBM's [X10](/wiki/X10_(programming_language) "X10 (programming language)").

Multi-core processing has also affected the ability of modern computational software development. Developers programming in newer languages might find that their modern languages do not support multi-core functionality. This then requires the use of [numerical libraries](/wiki/List_of_numerical_libraries "List of numerical libraries") to access code written in languages like [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)") and [Fortran](/wiki/Fortran "Fortran"), which perform math computations faster[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] than newer languages like [C#](/wiki/C_Sharp_(programming_language) "C Sharp (programming language)"). Intel's MKL and AMD's [ACML](/wiki/AMD_Core_Math_Library "AMD Core Math Library") are written in these native languages and take advantage of multi-core processing. Balancing the application workload across processors can be problematic, especially if they have different performance characteristics. There are different conceptual models to deal with the problem, for example using a coordination language and program building blocks (programming libraries or higher-order functions). Each block can have a different native implementation for each processor type. Users simply program using these abstractions and an intelligent compiler chooses the best implementation based on the context.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Managing [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrent_computing "Concurrent computing") acquires a central role in developing parallel applications. The basic steps in designing parallel applications are:

Partitioning
:   The partitioning stage of a design is intended to expose opportunities for parallel execution. Hence, the focus is on defining a large number of small tasks in order to yield what is termed a fine-grained decomposition of a problem.

Communication
:   The tasks generated by a partition are intended to execute concurrently but cannot, in general, execute independently. The computation to be performed in one task will typically require data associated with another task. Data must then be transferred between tasks so as to allow computation to proceed. This information flow is specified in the communication phase of a design.

Agglomeration
:   In the third stage, development moves from the abstract toward the concrete. Developers revisit decisions made in the partitioning and communication phases with a view to obtaining an algorithm that will execute efficiently on some class of parallel computer. In particular, developers consider whether it is useful to combine, or agglomerate, tasks identified by the partitioning phase, so as to provide a smaller number of tasks, each of greater size. They also determine whether it is worthwhile to replicate data and computation.

Mapping
:   In the fourth and final stage of the design of parallel algorithms, the developers specify where each task is to execute. This mapping problem does not arise on uniprocessors or on shared-memory computers that provide automatic task scheduling.

On the other hand, on the [server side](/wiki/Server-side "Server-side"), multi-core processors are ideal because they allow many users to connect to a site simultaneously and have independent [threads](/wiki/Thread_(computer_science) "Thread (computer science)") of execution. This allows for Web servers and application servers that have much better [throughput](/wiki/Throughput "Throughput").

### Licensing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Licensing")]

Vendors may license some software "per processor". This can give rise to ambiguity, because a "processor" may consist either of a single core or of a combination of cores.

* Initially, for some of its enterprise software, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") continued to use a per-[socket](/wiki/CPU_socket "CPU socket") licensing system. However, for some software such as [BizTalk Server 2013](/wiki/BizTalk "BizTalk"), [SQL Server 2014](/wiki/Microsoft_SQL_Server "Microsoft SQL Server"), and [Windows Server 2016](/wiki/Windows_Server_2016 "Windows Server 2016"), Microsoft has shifted to per-core licensing.[[27]](#cite_note-ars-2016license-27)
* [Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") counts an AMD X2 or an Intel dual-core CPU as a single processor[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but uses other metrics for other types, especially for processors with more than two cores.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Embedded applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Embedded applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/DHCOM_Computer_On_Module_-_AM35x.jpg/250px-DHCOM_Computer_On_Module_-_AM35x.jpg)](/wiki/File:DHCOM_Computer_On_Module_-_AM35x.jpg)

An *embedded system* on a plug-in card with processor, memory, power supply, and external interfaces

[Embedded computing](/wiki/Embedded_computing "Embedded computing") operates in an area of processor technology distinct from that of "mainstream" PCs. The same technological drives towards multi-core apply here too. Indeed, in many cases the application is a "natural" fit for multi-core technologies, if the task can easily be partitioned between the different processors.

In addition, embedded software is typically developed for a specific hardware release, making issues of [software portability](/wiki/Software_portability "Software portability"), legacy code or supporting independent developers less critical than is the case for PC or enterprise computing. As a result, it is easier for developers to adopt new technologies and as a result there is a greater variety of multi-core processing architectures and suppliers.

## Network processors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Network processors")]

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit), multi-core [network processors](/wiki/Network_processors "Network processors") have become mainstream, with companies such as [Freescale Semiconductor](/wiki/Freescale_Semiconductor "Freescale Semiconductor"), [Cavium Networks](/wiki/Cavium_Networks "Cavium Networks"), [Wintegra](/w/index.php?title=Wintegra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wintegra (page does not exist)") and [Broadcom](/wiki/Broadcom "Broadcom") all manufacturing products with eight processors. For the system developer, a key challenge is how to exploit all the cores in these devices to achieve maximum networking performance at the system level, despite the performance limitations inherent in a [symmetric multiprocessing](/wiki/Symmetric_multiprocessing "Symmetric multiprocessing") (SMP) operating system. Companies such as [6WIND](/wiki/6WIND "6WIND") provide portable packet processing software designed so that the networking data plane runs in a fast path environment outside the operating system of the network device.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Digital signal processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Digital signal processing")]

In [digital signal processing](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing") the same trend applies: [Texas Instruments](/wiki/Texas_Instruments "Texas Instruments") has the three-core TMS320C6488 and four-core TMS320C5441, [Freescale](/wiki/Freescale "Freescale") the four-core MSC8144 and six-core MSC8156 (and both have stated they are working on eight-core successors). Newer entries include the Storm-1 family from [Stream Processors, Inc](https://web.archive.org/web/20101210220716/http://www.streamprocessors.com/streamprocessors/Home.html) with 40 and 80 general purpose ALUs per chip, all programmable in C as a SIMD engine and [Picochip](/wiki/Picochip "Picochip") with 300 processors on a single die, focused on communication applications.

## Heterogeneous systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Heterogeneous systems")]

In [heterogeneous computing](/wiki/Heterogeneous_computing "Heterogeneous computing"), where a system uses more than one kind of processor or cores, multi-core solutions are becoming more common: [Xilinx](/wiki/Xilinx "Xilinx") Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC has a quad-core ARM Cortex-A53 and dual-core ARM Cortex-R5. Software solutions such as OpenAMP are being used to help with inter-processor communication.

Mobile devices may use the [ARM big.LITTLE](/wiki/ARM_big.LITTLE "ARM big.LITTLE") architecture.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Multi-core_processor&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#ref_DSP)** [Digital signal processors](/wiki/Digital_signal_processor "Digital signal processor") (DSPs) have used multi-core architectures for much longer than high-end general-purpose processors. A typical example of a DSP-specific implementation would be a combination of a [RISC](/wiki/Reduced_instruction_set_computing "Reduced instruction set computing") CPU and a DSP [MPU](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor"). This allows for the design of products that require a general-purpose processor for user interfaces and a DSP for real-time data processing; this type of design is common in [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone"). In other applications, a growing number of companies have developed multi-core DSPs with very large numbers of processors.
2. **[^](#ref_PMTandSMP)** Two types of [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") are able to use a dual-CPU multiprocessor: partitioned multiprocessing and [symmetric multiprocessing](/wiki/Symmetric_multiprocessing "Symmetric multiprocessing") (SMP). In a partitioned architecture, each CPU boots into separate segments of physical memory and operate independently; in an SMP OS, processors work in a shared space, executing threads within the OS independently.
